name: Bunratty Castle
description: The most complete and authentic medieval castle in Ireland, Bunratty has been restored with period furnishings. Its medieval banquets and adjacent folk park attract hundreds of thousands of visitors annually.
history: Bunratty Castle is a large 15th-century tower house in County Clare, Ireland. It is located in the centre of Bunratty village, by the N18 road between Limerick and Ennis, near Shannon Town and its airport. The castle and the adjoining folk park are run by Shannon Heritage as tourist attractions.

name: Dunguaire Castle
description: A picturesque tower house on the shores of Galway Bay near Kinvara, Dunguaire is one of Ireland's most photographed castles. It hosts medieval banquets in summer months.
history: Dunguaire Castle is a 16th-century tower house on the southeastern shore of Galway Bay in County Galway, Ireland, near Kinvara. The name derives from the dun (fort) of King Guaire, the legendary king of Connacht.

name: Ashford Castle
description: Originally a de Burgo Anglo-Norman castle, Ashford was expanded into a grand Victorian estate by the Guinness family. Now a five-star hotel, it sits between Lough Corrib and Lough Mask.
history: Ashford Castle is a mainly Victorian and medieval castle near Cong on the County Mayo–Galway border in Ireland. The castle has been expanded over the centuries and turned into a five star hotel. It is located on the County Galway side of Lough Corrib and was previously owned by the Guinness family.

name: Athenry Castle
description: A well-preserved Anglo-Norman castle built by Meiler de Bermingham around 1240. The town's medieval walls, among the best preserved in Ireland, still encircle the old town.
history: Athenry Castle is a tower house and National Monument located in Athenry, Ireland.

name: Portumna Castle
description: A semi-fortified Renaissance mansion built by the Earl of Clanricarde around 1618 on the shores of Lough Derg. Its formal gardens have been restored to their original geometric layout.
history: Portumna Castle is a semi-fortified house in Portumna, County Galway, Ireland which was built in the early 17th century by Richard Burke, 4th Earl of Clanricarde.

name: Thoor Ballylee
description: A Norman tower house famously owned by W.B. Yeats, who restored it as a summer home and wrote some of his greatest poetry here. It appears in several of his poems as a powerful symbol.
history: Thoor Ballylee Castle is a fortified, 15th-century Anglo-Norman tower house built by the septs de Burgo, or Burke, near the town of Gort in County Galway, Ireland. It is also known as Yeats's Tower because it was once owned and inhabited by the poet William Butler Yeats.

name: Lynch's Castle
description: One of the finest medieval town houses in Ireland, located on Shop Street in Galway city. Its carved stone façade features gargoyles and the Lynch family coat of arms.
history: Summerhill House was a 100-roomed Palladian house in County Meath, Ireland which was the ancestral seat of the Viscounts Langford and the Barons Langford. Built in 1731, it was likely designed by Sir Edward Lovett Pearce and completed by Richard Cassels in the Palladian style, although Sir John Vanbrugh, who was related to Pearce and with whom he trained, is thought to have also influenced the design of the house, which could be seen by the Baroque details, great arched chimney stacks and the palatial grandeur and scale.

name: Aughnanure Castle
description: A well-preserved six-storey tower house of the 'ferocious' O'Flaherty clan on the shores of Lough Corrib. Its unusual double bawn and watchtower make it one of Connacht's finest tower houses.
history: Aughnanure Castle is a tower house near Oughterard on the N59, in County Galway, in the west of Ireland. It was built by the O'Flaherty family in the late 15th century and fully restored in the 1960s. Today it is open to visitors from March to November.

name: Dún Aonghasa
description: A prehistoric stone fort perched on a 100-metre cliff edge on Inis Mór, the largest Aran Island. Its concentric stone walls and chevaux-de-frise defences date back over 3,000 years.
history: Dún Aonghasa is the best-known of several prehistoric hill forts on the Aran Islands of County Galway, Ireland. It lies on Inis Mór, at the edge of a 100-metre-high (330 ft) cliff.

name: Claregalway Friary
description: A Franciscan friary founded around 1252 on the River Clare. Its fine tower and transept window survive, and the building remained in use as a church until the 19th century.
history: The Claregalway Friary is a ruined medieval Franciscan abbey in Claregalway, County Galway, Ireland.

name: Kilmacduagh Monastery
description: Ireland's tallest round tower leans dramatically over a cluster of ruined churches at the edge of the Burren — a hauntingly beautiful 7th-century monastery lost in fields of limestone and wildflowers.
history: Founded in the 7th century by St Colman MacDuagh, who legend says built it where his belt fell to the ground in the Burren woods. The striking 34m round tower, Ireland's tallest, dates from the 10th-12th century and has a noticeable lean. The monastery became the seat of the Diocese of Kilmacduagh. Several churches spanning different centuries survive. Free and always open.

name: Dysert O'Dea Castle
description: A restored tower house in the Burren that now houses an archaeology centre and museum. Nearby stands a 12th-century Romanesque church doorway and high cross.
history: O'Dea Castle, also known as Dysert O'Dea Castle, is an Irish fortified tower house, loosely described as a castle at Dysert O'Dea, the former O'Dea clan stronghold, 5 kilometres (3 mi) from Corofin, County Clare. It was built between 1470 and 1490 by Diarmaid O'Dea, Lord of Cineal Fearmaic, and stands some 50 feet (15 m) high on a limestone outcrop base measuring 20 by 40 feet. The tower is adjacent to Dysert O'Dea Monastery, close to the R476 road.

name: Knappogue Castle
description: A McNamara tower house built in 1467, beautifully restored and hosting medieval banquets. Oliver Cromwell used it as his headquarters during the conquest of Munster.
history: Knappogue Castle is a tower house, built in 1467 and expanded in the mid-19th century, located in the parish of Quin, County Clare, Ireland. It has been restored and is open to guided tours.

name: Quin Abbey
description: One of Ireland's best-preserved Franciscan friaries, built within the walls of an earlier Norman castle. Its intact cloister is among the finest in the country.
history: Quin Abbey, is a ruined Franciscan abbey in Quin, County Clare, Ireland. It was built for Fathers Purcell and Mooney, friars of the Franciscan order.

name: Ennis Friary
description: A Franciscan friary founded by the O'Briens around 1241, noted for its superb sculptural decoration. Its McMahon tomb panels depict scenes from the Passion with vivid medieval artistry.
history: Ennis Friary was a Franciscan friary in the town of Ennis, County Clare, Ireland. It was established in the middle of the 13th century by the ruling O'Brien dynasty who supported it for most of its existence. Following the suppression of the monasteries in the 16th century, the friary continued to function for a while despite the loss of its lands. In the early 17th century, the buildings were handed over to the Church of Ireland as a place of worship. It was used as such until the late 19th century. After the construction of a new Church of Ireland building, the friary fell into ruin.

name: Sligo Abbey
description: A Dominican friary founded in 1253 by Maurice FitzGerald, noted for its beautifully carved high altar. Its cloister and chapter house contain fine examples of medieval Gothic sculpture.
history: Sligo Abbey was a Dominican convent in Sligo, Ireland, founded in 1253. It was built in the Romanesque style with some later additions and alterations. Extensive ruins remain, mainly of the church and the cloister.

name: Parke's Castle
description: A beautifully restored Plantation-era fortified manor house on the shores of Lough Gill. Built by Captain Robert Parke using stones from an earlier O'Rourke tower house.
history: Parke's Castle is a 17th-century Manor house. It was originally known as Newtowne and is situated on the shore of Lough Gill, County Leitrim, Ireland.

name: Kylemore Abbey
description: A neo-Gothic fairytale on a Connemara lakeshore — built by a heartbroken husband for his beloved wife, now home to Benedictine nuns in one of Ireland's most photographed settings.
history: Built 1867-1871 by Mitchell Henry, a wealthy London doctor and politician, as a gift for his wife Margaret. After Margaret died of dysentery in Egypt in 1874, Henry built a miniature Gothic cathedral on the grounds in her memory. The estate was purchased by Benedictine nuns fleeing Belgium during WWI in 1920. The nuns ran a prestigious boarding school until 2010. The Victorian Walled Garden, restored from 2000, is the largest in Ireland.

name: Ballymote Castle
description: The last and most symmetrical of Connacht's great Norman castles — a massive keepless enclosure with imposing corner towers, built to protect a conquered province. Even in ruin, its geometric perfection is startlingly modern.
history: Built c.1300 by Richard Óg de Burgh, 2nd Earl of Ulster, to protect his newly won possessions in County Sligo. The most symmetrical of all Irish keepless castles, with similarities to Beaumaris. Fought over during the Nine Years' War. National Monument managed by the Office of Public Works.

name: Easkey Castle
description: Windswept ruins of an O'Dowd stronghold on the wild Sligo coast, where Atlantic gales have sculpted both stone and legend for centuries.
history: Originally an O'Dowd castle dating from the 15th century, Easkey Castle stands near the famous surfing beach of Easkey in County Sligo. The O'Dowds were chiefs of Tireragh. The castle was a centre of Gaelic power before falling to English control. Today the atmospheric ruin overlooks some of Ireland's best surf breaks.

name: Moygara Castle
description: A massive and mysterious O'Gara stronghold hidden in the Sligo countryside — four corner towers frame a forgotten courtyard where Gaelic chieftains once held sway.
history: A large castle ruin in County Sligo, built by the O'Gara clan who were chieftains of Coolavin. Features four substantial corner towers and curtain walls enclosing a large courtyard. One of the most impressive and least-visited Gaelic castle ruins in Ireland.

name: Ross Errilly Friary
description: The largest and best-preserved medieval Franciscan friary in Ireland, a sprawling monastic complex where cloisters, kitchens, and fishpond survive in extraordinary detail.
history: Founded around 1351 and substantially rebuilt in the 15th century, Ross Errilly near Headford is the largest Franciscan friary in Ireland. Its remarkably complete remains include a double nave church, a beautiful cloister, a kitchen with fish tank, bakery, and dormitories. The friars were repeatedly expelled but always returned; the last friar died here in 1753.

name: Ballinasloe Castle
description: Faded remnants of a medieval stronghold in the great fair town of Ballinasloe, where the River Suck once carried commerce and conflict in equal measure.
history: Ballinasloe Castle dates to the Norman period and was held by various families including the O'Kellys. The town became famous for its great October horse fair, one of the oldest and largest in Europe. Little remains of the castle today, but the strategic crossing of the River Suck ensured its military significance throughout the medieval period.

name: Oranmore Castle
description: A 15th-century tower house perched at the edge of Galway Bay, where Clanricarde lords once held out against Confederate rebels and writer Anita Leslie later threw legendary parties. Restored from ruin, it's a living piece of Wild Atlantic heritage.
history: Built c.15th century, possibly on an older fortification. Used as a Clanricarde stronghold. During the 1642 Confederate rebellion, the 5th Earl held the castle against rebels. Abandoned in the 19th century. Purchased by writer Anita Leslie in 1947 for £200 and reroofed. Now owned by her daughter Leonie.

name: Tyrone House
description: A magnificent Georgian ruin overlooking Galway Bay—its roofless grandeur a haunting monument to the rise and catastrophic fall of the St George family.
history: Built in 1779 by Christopher St George, Tyrone House was a grand Georgian mansion overlooking Galway Bay. The family fell into debt and the house was used as a workhouse during the Great Famine. Burned by anti-Treaty forces in 1920, its spectacular shell remains one of Ireland's most dramatically ruined country houses.

name: Ballyportry Castle
description: A beautifully restored tower house in the shadow of the Burren, offering luxury accommodation within authentic 15th-century walls.
history: Built in the 15th century, Ballyportry Castle is a fine tower house in the Burren region of County Clare. It has been carefully restored and operates as exclusive holiday accommodation, allowing guests to experience medieval castle life amid the extraordinary limestone landscape of the Burren. The castle retains many original features including spiral staircases and vaulted ceilings.

name: Newtown Castle
description: A uniquely designed round tower house perched on a square base in the Burren, now a beautifully restored cultural centre and gallery.
history: Built in the 16th century, Newtown Castle near Ballyvaughan is architecturally unique in Ireland—a round tower house on a square pyramidal base. Restored in the 1990s by the Burren College of Art, it now serves as a gallery and cultural space. The unusual design reflects both defensive needs and the status of its O'Brien builders.

name: Doonagore Castle
description: Ireland's most photographed tower house, its perfect silhouette crowning the cliffs above Doolin with the Aran Islands shimmering on the Atlantic horizon.
history: Built in the 16th century, Doonagore Castle is a round tower house perched dramatically on the cliffs above Doolin in County Clare. In 1588, survivors from the Spanish Armada shipwrecks were held here before being executed. Privately restored in the 20th century, the castle is not open to visitors but its iconic profile against the Atlantic sky makes it one of Ireland's most photographed buildings.

name: Ballinalacken Castle
description: A crumbling O'Brien tower house perched on a Burren hilltop, with sweeping views from the Cliffs of Moher to the Aran Islands and Galway Bay.
history: Built by the O'Brien clan in the 15th century, Ballinalacken Castle commands one of the finest panoramic positions in County Clare. The four-storey tower house stands on the edge of the Burren's limestone karst landscape. A Victorian country house was built beside it and now operates as a hotel. The castle ruins are freely accessible in the hotel grounds.

name: Dromoland Castle
description: A baronial castle turned five-star hotel on the shores of Lough Dromoland — ancestral seat of Irish high kings, where ancient lineage meets luxurious escape in County Clare.
history: The ancestral home of the O'Brien family, direct descendants of Brian Boru, High King of Ireland who defeated the Vikings at the Battle of Clontarf in 1014. The current neo-Gothic castle was built in the 1820s-1830s, replacing an earlier tower house. Converted into a luxury hotel in 1963. The 450-acre estate features an 18-hole golf course, lake, and walled garden. One of Ireland's most prestigious castle hotels.

name: Danganbrack Castle
description: A lonely tower house ruin in the Clare countryside, its crumbling walls slowly returning to the limestone landscape from which they were quarried.
history: Danganbrack Castle is a 15th-century tower house in the Burren region of County Clare. Built by a local clan, possibly the O'Briens or McNamaras, the modest tower house is now in a ruinous state but representative of the hundreds of such structures that once dotted the Irish landscape. It stands in farmland with limited access.

name: Gleninagh Castle
description: A striking O'Loughlin tower house clinging to the wild Burren coast, its stark walls rising above Black Head where limestone meets the Atlantic.
history: Built in the early 16th century by the O'Loughlin clan, Gleninagh Castle occupies a dramatic position on the coast road beneath Black Head in the Burren, County Clare. The four-storey tower house controlled the coastal route and trade. It was forfeited during the Cromwellian period. The castle's dramatic setting, with the grey Burren hills behind and Galway Bay before it, makes it one of Clare's most evocative ruins.

name: Mooghaun Fort
description: One of the largest prehistoric hillforts in Europe, its massive stone ramparts enclosing a vast area atop a hill overlooking the Shannon Estuary.
history: Dating to the Late Bronze Age (around 900 BC), Mooghaun Fort near Newmarket-on-Fergus is one of the largest hillforts in Europe, with three concentric stone walls enclosing over 12 hectares. In 1854, the Great Clare Gold Hoard—the largest collection of Bronze Age gold ever found in Western Europe—was discovered nearby. The fort was likely a major ceremonial and defensive centre.

name: Ballynahinch Castle
description: A lakeside castle hotel deep in the wilds of Connemara — where salmon leap in the river, ancient woodland whispers, and the ghosts of pirate queen Grace O'Malley still linger.
history: Set on a 700-acre estate in the heart of Connemara, with origins as a stronghold of the O'Flaherty clan. Connected to the legendary pirate queen Grace O'Malley (Granuaile) through her marriage to Donal O'Flaherty. Later owned by Richard Martin, known as 'Humanity Dick' for founding the RSPCA. Indian maharajah Ranjitsinhji, the famous cricketer, purchased the estate in 1924. Now a luxury castle hotel renowned for its salmon and sea trout fishing on the Ballynahinch River.

name: Clifden Castle
description: Gothic ruins of John D'Arcy's ambitious mansion overlooking Clifden Bay, a haunting monument to the man who founded Connemara's capital and died in its Famine.
history: Built around 1818 by John D'Arcy, founder of Clifden town, this Gothic Revival manor house commanded spectacular views over Clifden Bay and the Twelve Bens mountains. D'Arcy poured his fortune into developing Clifden but died bankrupt. The castle was sold and eventually abandoned, its ruins now freely accessible via a scenic coastal walk from Clifden town.

name: Fiddaun Castle
description: One of Ireland's largest and best-preserved tower houses, this massive O'Shaughnessy fortress dominates the south Galway landscape from behind its double bawn walls.
history: Built in the late 15th or early 16th century by the O'Shaughnessy clan, Fiddaun Castle is among the largest tower houses in Ireland. The castle features an impressive double bawn wall—an inner and outer enclosure—and a tall tower with fine architectural details. The O'Shaughnessys held the castle until the Cromwellian confiscations.

name: Annaghdown Castle
description: Lakeside ruins of a medieval ecclesiastical stronghold on the shores of Lough Corrib, where bishops and chieftains once vied for spiritual and temporal power.
history: Annaghdown on the shore of Lough Corrib was an important ecclesiastical site from the 6th century, associated with St Brendan the Navigator. The castle dates to the medieval period and was linked to the diocese of Annaghdown. Nearby are the ruins of a cathedral, priory, and churches spanning several centuries of Christian worship.

name: Menlo Castle
description: A hauntingly beautiful ivy-cloaked ruin on the banks of the River Corrib, destroyed by a devastating fire that claimed the lady of the house in 1910.
history: Home to the Blake family for over 400 years, Menlo Castle was a 16th-century tower house expanded into a substantial mansion. In 1910, a catastrophic fire gutted the building, killing the elderly Eleanor Blake. The ruins, smothered in ivy beside the River Corrib just outside Galway city, are among the most atmospheric and photographed castle ruins in the west of Ireland.

name: Clonbur Castle
description: Quiet ruins of a medieval tower in the scenic lakeland between Lough Corrib and Lough Mask, where Connemara's wildness meets the fertile Joyce Country.
history: Clonbur Castle is a medieval tower house in the village of Clonbur, County Galway, situated in the scenic corridor between Lough Corrib and Lough Mask. The area was historically part of the Joyce Country, named after the Welsh-Norman Joyce family who settled here in the 13th century. The castle remains stand in the village as a reminder of medieval settlement in this lakeland district.

name: Moyode Castle
description: A magnificent ruin of a Persse family mansion in east Galway, its castellated Victorian towers and sweeping demesne a ghost of Ascendancy grandeur.
history: Moyode Castle near Craughwell was the seat of the Persse family, prominent Galway landlords. The original tower house was incorporated into a grand Victorian castellated mansion. The family was noted for their fox hunting and Lady Augusta Gregory (nee Persse) of nearby Coole Park was related. The house was burned during the Civil War in 1922 and stands as an impressive ruin.

name: Killarone Castle
description: A modest tower house ruin in the Galway countryside, one of countless small medieval fortifications that once studded the landscape of Connacht.
history: Killarone Castle is a small medieval tower house in County Galway, typical of the hundreds built by minor chieftains and landowners across Connacht between the 14th and 16th centuries. These compact defensive residences reflected the turbulent conditions of medieval Ireland where every family of means required a fortified home.

name: Lydacan Castle
description: A hidden medieval tower house in the Galway countryside, its quiet decay among the fields a testament to the countless small strongholds that once networked Irish power.
history: Lydacan Castle is a medieval tower house in County Galway. Like many similar structures in the west of Ireland, it served as a fortified residence for a local family during the turbulent 15th and 16th centuries. The tower retains some structural integrity though largely ruinous, standing in agricultural land.

name: Merlin Park Castle
description: A striking ruined mansion on the eastern fringe of Galway city, its Gothic towers rising from a public woodland park popular with walkers and runners.
history: Merlin Park was the seat of the Blake family, one of the Fourteen Tribes of Galway. The castle evolved from a medieval tower house into a substantial mansion, which was damaged by fire. The grounds were acquired by the state and now form a public park adjoining University Hospital Galway, with woodland walks and the castle ruins as a centrepiece.

name: Rockfleet Castle
description: The iconic tower house of Grace O'Malley, the Pirate Queen of Connacht, standing defiant on the shores of Clew Bay where she once commanded her fleet.
history: Built in the mid-16th century, Rockfleet (Carrickahowley) Castle was the stronghold of Grace O'Malley (Granuaile), Ireland's legendary pirate queen. She famously received the English governor Sir Richard Bingham here and later sailed to London to negotiate with Queen Elizabeth I. The four-storey tower house stands on the edge of Clew Bay with commanding views across the water.

name: Burrishoole Abbey
description: Hauntingly beautiful Dominican friary ruins on the shores of an inlet near Newport, where medieval arches frame views of Clew Bay and Croagh Patrick.
history: Founded in 1469 by Richard Burke, Burrishoole Abbey is a Dominican friary on the shores of an inlet of Clew Bay near Newport, County Mayo. The well-preserved ruins include a church with fine tracery windows, a cloister, and domestic buildings. Grace O'Malley is said to have worshipped here. The setting, with Croagh Patrick visible beyond, is sublimely beautiful.

name: Clare Island Castle
description: Grace O'Malley's island fortress commanding the entrance to Clew Bay, where Ireland's most famous female warrior held court over her maritime empire.
history: Clare Island Castle, also known as Granuaile's Castle, was a stronghold of Grace O'Malley (Granuaile), the 16th-century pirate queen who controlled the seas off the west coast of Ireland. The tower house guards the harbour of Clare Island at the entrance to Clew Bay. O'Malley is traditionally buried in the nearby Cistercian abbey on the island.

name: Kilconly Tower
description: A modest medieval tower in the rural Mayo landscape, one of the scattered fortifications that once dotted the western seaboard.
history: Kilconly Tower is a medieval tower house in County Mayo, representative of the many small fortified residences built by local chieftains in the west of Ireland. These towers served both as residences and symbols of local authority in a landscape where power was fragmented among numerous clans.

name: Castlebar Castle
description: Scant remains of a Bingham stronghold in the county town of Mayo, where the 'Races of Castlebar' saw French and Irish rebels rout the British in 1798.
history: Castlebar Castle was built by the Bingham family, English administrators in Connacht, in the late 16th century. The town is most famous for the 1798 Battle of Castlebar, known as the 'Races of Castlebar,' when French forces under General Humbert and their Irish allies routed the British garrison in a humiliating defeat. Little of the castle survives today.

name: Kinlough Castle
description: Quiet ruins in the north Leitrim village of Kinlough, nestled between Lough Melvin and the Dartry Mountains on the Donegal border.
history: Kinlough Castle in County Leitrim stands in the village of Kinlough near the shores of Lough Melvin. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with the O'Rourke clan, Lords of Breifne. The area is renowned for Lough Melvin's unique fish species and the dramatic Dartry Mountain backdrop.

name: Moyne Abbey
description: One of Ireland's most complete Franciscan friaries, its soaring six-storey tower and pristine cloisters standing timelessly on the banks of the River Moy.
history: Founded around 1460 by the Burke family, Moyne Abbey near Killala in County Mayo is one of the best-preserved Franciscan friaries in Ireland. The remains include a magnificent six-storey tower, beautiful cloister, refectory, kitchen, and dormitories. The abbey was burned by Sir Richard Bingham in 1590 but the friars continued to use it secretly until the 18th century.

name: Rosserk Friary
description: A remarkably intact Franciscan friary on the Moy estuary, featuring Ireland's only surviving medieval piscina decorated with a round tower carving.
history: Founded around 1460, Rosserk Friary on the River Moy near Killala is one of the best-preserved Franciscan friaries in Ireland. It features a unique double piscina carved with a round tower motif—the only such example known in Ireland. Burned by Sir Richard Bingham in 1590, the remarkably complete ruins include church, cloister, domestic buildings, and a beautiful riverside setting.

name: Manorhamilton Castle
description: The substantial ruins of a Scottish planter's castle in the Leitrim highlands, its massive walls a monument to the ambitions of the Ulster Plantation.
history: Built in 1634 by Sir Frederick Hamilton, a Scottish planter, Manorhamilton Castle gave its name to the town that grew around it. Hamilton was a notoriously ruthless figure who sacked Sligo Abbey in 1642. The castle was captured by Confederate forces during the 1641 Rebellion and eventually fell into ruin. Its substantial remains dominate the town square.

name: Ballintubber Abbey
description: The abbey that refused to die — founded by an Irish king in 1216, suppressed, burned, roofless for centuries, yet never once stopped holding Mass through 800 years of defiance.
history: Founded in 1216 by King Cathal Crobdearg Ua Conchobair for the Augustinians. Suppressed and damaged during the Protestant Reformation, but the roofless abbey continued to be used by Catholics throughout the Penal times. The nave was restored and re-roofed in 1966 for the 750th anniversary. The Chapter House was restored in 1997. Features modern outdoor Stations of the Cross and a permanent Crib.

name: Cong Abbey
description: A Royal Abbey in Ireland's lake country — where the last High King of Ireland retreated and Romanesque stonework whispers of ancient devotion.
history: Originally founded in the 7th century by Saint Feichin, rebuilt in the 12th century as an Augustinian house. Turlough Mór O'Connor, last High King of Ireland, retired and died here in 1198. Features some of Ireland's finest Romanesque and early Gothic carved stonework. The Monks' Fishing House on the river is iconic.

name: Clontuskert Abbey
description: A hidden gem of medieval Ireland — a ruined Augustinian priory where a remarkable carved west doorway survives, alive with saints, animals, and Green Men.
history: An Augustinian priory founded in the 12th century near Ballinasloe, County Galway. Famous for its exceptionally carved Romanesque west doorway featuring figures of saints, animals, and Green Men. The ruins include parts of the nave, chancel, and cloister.

name: Kilconnell Friary
description: One of Ireland's finest Franciscan ruins — where exquisite carved tombs and a soaring Gothic window frame centuries of devotion in the Galway countryside.
history: A Franciscan friary founded around 1353 in Kilconnell, County Galway. Features beautifully carved medieval tombs and an impressive Gothic window. The friary was suppressed during the dissolution of the monasteries but the friars returned and maintained a presence for centuries.

name: Muckinish Castle
description: A small tower house ruin in the heart of the Burren, surrounded by the extraordinary limestone pavement and wildflowers of this unique landscape.
history: Muckinish Castle is a tower house in the Burren, County Clare, dating to the 15th or 16th century. Built by a local clan, it is one of many such towers that dot the Burren landscape. The castle's setting amid the karst limestone makes it a distinctive feature of this UNESCO-listed geopark region.

name: Finavarra Castle
description: A coastal tower house ruin near the Flaggy Shore in the Burren, where Seamus Heaney found poetic inspiration in the meeting of limestone and sea.
history: Finavarra Castle is a medieval tower house on the coast of the Burren in County Clare, near the Flaggy Shore made famous by Seamus Heaney's poem 'Postscript.' The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and was likely built by the O'Briens or another local clan. Its coastal setting in the Burren makes it a unique heritage site.

name: Rossroe Castle
description: A small tower house on the northern edge of the Burren, overlooking the blue waters of Galway Bay and the distant Connemara mountains.
history: Rossroe Castle is a medieval tower house in the Burren region of County Clare, overlooking Galway Bay. The compact tower dates to the 15th or 16th century and was built by a local clan. Its position on the Burren coast provides fine views across to Connemara and the Aran Islands.

name: Ballymulcashel Castle
description: A ruined tower house in the Clare countryside near Lough Derg, standing quietly amid the rolling farmland of east Clare.
history: Ballymulcashel Castle is a medieval tower house in County Clare near the shores of Lough Derg. Built in the 15th or 16th century, it was one of many such fortified residences in the area controlled by the O'Brien and McNamara clans. The tower retains partial walls and stands in agricultural land.

name: Caherkinmonwee Castle
description: A small fortification in the Burren limestone landscape, where medieval stonework merges with the ancient karst terrain in an almost seamless union.
history: Caherkinmonwee is a medieval fortified site in the Burren, County Clare. The structure blends into the limestone landscape characteristic of the Burren region. Like many fortifications in this area, it likely dates to the medieval period and served as a local stronghold for one of the Clare clans.

name: Mountcashel Castle
description: Ruins of a once-significant stronghold near Ennis, associated with the McNamara clan who controlled much of medieval Clare.
history: Mountcashel Castle in County Clare was associated with the McNamara clan, one of the principal families of the region. The castle dates to the medieval period and served as a local power centre. The McNamaras held extensive territories across Clare and built numerous tower houses and fortifications.

name: Kilkishen Castle
description: A medieval tower house in the east Clare countryside, one of the many small strongholds that once studded this landscape of clan rivalries.
history: Kilkishen Castle is a medieval tower house in County Clare. The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and was likely built by one of the local clans—the McNamaras or O'Briens—who controlled different parts of Clare. The tower stands in the rural landscape of east Clare.

name: Moghane Castle
description: A quiet ruin in the Clare lowlands, standing near the ancient hillfort of Mooghaun where the greatest Bronze Age gold hoard in western Europe was discovered.
history: Moghane Castle is a medieval tower house near Newmarket-on-Fergus in County Clare. It stands close to the great prehistoric hillfort of Mooghaun, near where the famous Great Clare Gold Hoard was discovered in 1854. The castle dates to the medieval period and was part of the network of fortifications in the Thomond region.

name: Ballyallia Castle
description: A fine McNamara tower house overlooking Ballyallia Lake near Ennis, a well-preserved medieval landmark in the Clare capital's green hinterland.
history: Ballyallia Castle near Ennis, County Clare, is a well-preserved tower house dating to the 15th century. Built by the McNamara clan, it overlooks Ballyallia Lake, a popular walking and birdwatching destination. The castle retains much of its original structure and is a prominent landmark on the approach to Ennis from the north.

name: Clarecastle Tower
description: A medieval tower in the village of Clarecastle, once a Norman stronghold controlling the route southward from Ennis along the River Fergus.
history: Clarecastle was an important Norman settlement on the River Fergus south of Ennis. The tower dates to the medieval period and was part of the Norman presence in Thomond. The de Clare family, who gave the county its name, established their base here. Thomas de Clare was killed at the Battle of Dysert O'Dea in 1318, ending Norman power in Clare.

name: Rosslevan Castle
description: A small medieval tower near Ennis, another thread in Clare's extraordinarily dense tapestry of tower houses and medieval fortifications.
history: Rosslevan Castle is a medieval tower house near Ennis, County Clare. Like many of the dozens of tower houses in the area, it was built by a local clan family in the 15th or 16th century. Clare has one of the highest concentrations of tower houses in Ireland, reflecting the wealth and competitiveness of the local clans.

name: Cahermurphy Castle
description: A tower house in the rolling west Clare landscape, one of the McNamara strongholds that once formed a chain of power across the county.
history: Cahermurphy Castle is a medieval tower house in west Clare. Built by the McNamara clan in the 15th or 16th century, it formed part of their extensive network of fortifications across the county. The tower stands in the rural landscape between Ennis and the Atlantic coast.

name: Clonderalaw Castle
description: A medieval tower house in the wild Shannon estuary landscape of west Clare, commanding views across the great river's wide mouth.
history: Clonderalaw Castle is a medieval tower house in west Clare overlooking the Shannon Estuary. Built in the 15th or 16th century, likely by a branch of the McNamara or O'Brien clans, it controlled territory along the estuary. The castle's setting in the estuary landscape provides wide views across the Shannon.

name: Carrigaholt Castle
description: A magnificent 15th-century tower house commanding the mouth of the Shannon, where dolphins now play beneath the walls that once watched for Spanish sails.
history: Built by the McMahon clan in the late 15th century, Carrigaholt Castle guards the entrance to the Shannon Estuary in west Clare. The impressive five-storey tower house was garrisoned by Spanish Armada survivors in 1588 before being captured by Cromwellian forces. Today the castle overlooks a harbour famous for dolphin-watching boat trips in the estuary.

name: Doonbeg Castle
description: Ruins of a tower house in the west Clare village of Doonbeg, guarding the mouth of the Doonbeg River where it meets the wild Atlantic.
history: Doonbeg Castle is a 16th-century tower house at the mouth of the Doonbeg River in west Clare. The castle was built by the Turlough clan and later held by various families. The village of Doonbeg sits between the Atlantic coast and the rolling Clare countryside. The nearby Trump International Golf Links has brought modern fame to the area.

name: Kilrush Tower House
description: A medieval tower in the heritage town of Kilrush, gateway to the Shannon Estuary and the mysterious monastic island of Scattery.
history: Kilrush Tower House stands in the west Clare town of Kilrush, which served as an important port on the Shannon Estuary. The tower dates to the medieval period and reflects the town's strategic significance. Kilrush is the departure point for boats to Scattery Island, with its remarkable cluster of monastic remains and round tower.

name: Knockfin Castle
description: A remote tower house ruin in the Galway countryside, standing watch over the vast boglands and stone-walled fields of east Connacht.
history: Knockfin Castle is a medieval tower house in County Galway. Like many such structures in the west, it was built by a local clan family as a fortified residence during the 15th or 16th century. The tower stands in the agricultural landscape of eastern Galway.

name: Derryhivenny Castle
description: A beautifully preserved tower house in east Galway, its imposing five-storey bulk and fine architectural details reflecting the wealth of the O'Madden clan.
history: Built in 1643 by Daniel O'Madden, Derryhivenny Castle near Portumna is one of the last tower houses built in Ireland. The fine five-storey tower features excellent stone carvings and a notable fortified gatehouse. It represents the final flowering of the Irish tower house tradition before the Cromwellian conquest transformed the country's architecture.

name: Kilcornan Castle
description: A quiet medieval tower in the Galway countryside, one of the chain of fortified residences that once controlled the fertile lowlands east of the city.
history: Kilcornan Castle is a medieval tower house in County Galway. Built in the 15th or 16th century by a local clan family, it was one of many such structures in the productive farmland east of Galway city. The tower retains partial walls and stands in agricultural land.

name: Caherlistrane Castle
description: A tower house ruin in the Galway village of Caherlistrane, in the fertile plain between Lough Corrib and Tuam.
history: Caherlistrane Castle is a medieval tower house in County Galway. The village sits in the fertile agricultural plain between Lough Corrib and Tuam. The castle was built by a local clan family and served as a fortified residence during the turbulent medieval period.

name: Moylough Castle
description: Medieval castle remains in the east Galway village of Moylough, where the famous Moylough Belt Shrine was discovered in nearby bogland.
history: Moylough Castle is a medieval fortification in County Galway. The village is famous for the discovery of the Moylough Belt Shrine, an 8th-century decorated bronze belt reliquary now in the National Museum of Ireland. The castle dates to the medieval period and served as a local stronghold.

name: Monivea Castle
description: Ruins of a Ffrenche family castle in east Galway, surrounded by a beautiful demesne with an arboretum and nature trail.
history: Monivea Castle was the seat of the Ffrenche family, one of the Fourteen Tribes of Galway. The castle evolved from a medieval tower house into a substantial mansion that was burned in 1826 and rebuilt. The estate features a notable arboretum and woodland walks. The nearby village preserves the planned estate village character typical of the Big House era.

name: Ballinderry Castle
description: A tower house ruin in east Galway, standing quietly in the pastoral landscape that stretches between Loughrea and the Shannon plain.
history: Ballinderry Castle is a medieval tower house in County Galway. Built in the 15th or 16th century, it was one of numerous tower houses in the productive farmland of east Galway. The castle served as a fortified residence for a local family during the medieval period.

name: Loughrea Castle
description: Fragmentary Norman remains in the heritage town of Loughrea, once the de Burgh stronghold that controlled the eastern approaches to Galway.
history: Loughrea Castle was built by Richard de Burgh in the 13th century as part of the Norman conquest of Connacht. The town grew around the castle and became an important Anglo-Norman settlement. The town walls, a Carmelite monastery, and the remarkable St Brendan's Cathedral (with its outstanding collection of Celtic Revival stained glass) all stem from this medieval foundation.

name: Garbally Castle
description: Ruins of a grand estate near Ballinasloe, once seat of the Clancarty family and now part of a school campus in east Galway.
history: Garbally Castle near Ballinasloe was the seat of the Le Poer Trench family, Earls of Clancarty. The original castle dates to the medieval period, with the main house rebuilt in the 18th and 19th centuries. The estate now houses Garbally College, a secondary school. The grounds retain parkland character and some historical features.

name: Clonbrock Castle
description: A melancholy Victorian ruin in east Galway, its crumbling towers rising from an overgrown demesne that was once a centre of Anglo-Irish society.
history: Clonbrock House near Ahascragh was the seat of the Dillon family, Lords Clonbrock. The house evolved from a medieval castle into an impressive Victorian mansion. The family's remarkable photographic archive, documenting Irish Big House life, is now in the National Library. The house fell into ruin after being sold in the 20th century.

name: Cloghanower Castle
description: A remote tower house in the wilds of Connemara, one of the few medieval fortifications in this rugged, stone-walled landscape.
history: Cloghanower Castle is a tower house in the Connemara region of County Galway. Medieval fortifications are rare in Connemara due to its harsh terrain and sparse population. The tower dates to the 15th or 16th century and likely served as a residence for a local O'Flaherty chieftain.

name: Renvyle Castle
description: Haunting remains of an O'Flaherty castle on the wild Renvyle Peninsula, later owned by Oliver St John Gogarty, the wit and model for Buck Mulligan.
history: Renvyle Castle on the tip of the Renvyle Peninsula in Connemara was an O'Flaherty stronghold. It later became the property of Oliver St John Gogarty, the surgeon, poet, and wit who was James Joyce's model for Buck Mulligan in Ulysses. Gogarty converted the castle into a hotel; it burned in the Civil War but was rebuilt. Renvyle House Hotel still operates today.

name: Hen's Castle
description: A mysterious island fortress on Lough Corrib, where Grace O'Malley allegedly defended the walls while her defenders wavered—hence 'the Hen's Castle.'
history: Hen's Castle (Caislean na Circe) sits on a small island in Lough Corrib. According to legend, Grace O'Malley defended the castle so fiercely when men refused to fight that it was renamed in her honour. The castle dates to the 13th century and was originally an O'Flaherty stronghold. Its island setting makes it difficult to access but atmospheric to view.

name: Carna Castle
description: A modest castle ruin in the Connemara Gaeltacht, where the Irish language and ancient traditions survive amid one of Europe's wildest coastal landscapes.
history: Carna Castle is in the heart of the Connemara Gaeltacht (Irish-speaking area) in County Galway. The castle dates to the medieval period and is associated with local clan families. The Carna area is renowned for its strong Irish language tradition, traditional music, and spectacular coastal scenery.

name: Ardamullivan Castle
description: A substantial and well-preserved tower house deep in the Burren, its pale limestone walls emerging from the moonscape of ancient rock like a natural formation.
history: Ardamullivan Castle is a well-preserved 16th-century tower house in the Burren, County Galway. Built by the O'Shaughnessy clan, the four-storey tower retains much of its original fabric and sits in a dramatic Burren landscape of limestone pavement and hazel scrub. It is one of the finest tower houses in the Burren region.

name: Pallas Castle
description: A striking tower house in the south Galway countryside, its walls rising sheer from farmland at the edge of the Burren.
history: Pallas Castle is a medieval tower house in County Galway near the Burren border. Built by the Burke family in the 16th century, the tall tower retains good structural integrity. It was part of the network of fortified residences controlled by the various branches of the powerful de Burgh (Burke) family in Connacht.

name: Ballybrit Castle
description: A tower house near the famous Galway Racecourse, where medieval heritage meets the thunder of hooves during the legendary Galway Races.
history: Ballybrit Castle is a medieval tower house on the eastern outskirts of Galway city, near Ballybrit Racecourse, home of the famous Galway Races held each July/August. The castle dates to the medieval period and is one of numerous tower houses that once ringed the medieval city of Galway.

name: Rahasane Castle
description: A medieval tower ruin in the south Galway turlough country, surrounded by the seasonally flooding lakes that make this landscape unique in Europe.
history: Rahasane Castle is a medieval tower house near Rahasane in County Galway. The area is notable for its turloughs—seasonal lakes that fill in winter and drain in summer, a phenomenon unique to Ireland's limestone regions. The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and served as a local stronghold.

name: Carrickkildavnet Castle
description: Grace O'Malley's Achill Island tower house, standing on a rocky shore where the Pirate Queen commanded the seas between Clare Island and the mainland.
history: Carrickkildavnet Castle on Achill Island, County Mayo, was a stronghold of Grace O'Malley (Granuaile), the 16th-century pirate queen. The tower house stands on the southern coast of Achill with commanding views across Achill Sound. It was a key position in O'Malley's control of the western seaways and remains an iconic Achill landmark.

name: Ballylahan Castle
description: A medieval bridge-castle spanning the River Moy in County Mayo, an unusual and picturesque combination of bridge and fortified tower.
history: Ballylahan Castle near Foxford in County Mayo is an unusual medieval structure combining a castle with a bridge over the River Moy. Built by the Jordan (Mac Siurtain) family in the 13th century, this castle-on-a-bridge is a rare type in Ireland. The remaining tower stands on one of the bridge piers over the river.

name: Turlough Round Tower
description: A fine early Christian round tower beside the historic Turlough village, now home to the National Museum of Country Life.
history: The Round Tower at Turlough, County Mayo, dates to the 12th century and stands beside the ruins of a medieval church. The nearby Turlough Park House, a Victorian Gothic mansion, now houses the National Museum of Ireland - Country Life, displaying rural Irish heritage. The round tower and church ruins add medieval atmosphere to the museum grounds.

name: Castlecarra
description: A ruined medieval tower on the shores of Lough Carra in County Mayo, overlooking one of Ireland's clearest and most beautiful limestone lakes.
history: Castlecarra is a medieval tower house on the shores of Lough Carra in County Mayo. Lough Carra is renowned as one of Ireland's most pristine and ecologically important lakes, with remarkably clear water and a rich archaeological landscape. The castle dates to the medieval period and was held by local Mayo families.

name: Ballinrobe Castle
description: Medieval castle remains in the south Mayo market town of Ballinrobe, where Captain Boycott's infamous treatment of tenants gave the English language a new word.
history: Ballinrobe Castle in County Mayo dates to the medieval period and is associated with the de Burgh (Burke) family's Norman expansion into Connacht. The town later gained fame as the home of Captain Charles Boycott, the land agent whose ostracism by local tenants in 1880 gave the word 'boycott' to the English language.

name: Shrule Castle
description: A Norman castle at a historic crossing of the River Black in south Mayo, where three counties meet and medieval frontier meets modern Ireland.
history: Shrule Castle in County Mayo was built at a strategic crossing of the River Black near the junction of counties Mayo, Galway, and Roscommon. The Norman castle dates to the 13th century and controlled this important route. In 1642, a tragic massacre of Protestant settlers occurred near Shrule during the Confederate Wars.

name: Castleaffy
description: A quiet tower house in the Mayo countryside, one of the scattered medieval fortifications built by the Burke clan across Connacht.
history: Castleaffy is a medieval tower house in County Mayo associated with the Burke (de Burgh) family. The Burkes were the dominant Anglo-Norman family in Connacht and built numerous castles across Mayo and Galway. This tower dates to the medieval period and served as a local Burke residence.

name: Belcarra Castle
description: A tower house ruin near the charming Mayo village of Belcarra, associated with the family of Margaret Burke Sheridan, Ireland's greatest opera diva.
history: Belcarra Castle is a medieval tower house near the village of Belcarra in County Mayo. The village is associated with Margaret Burke Sheridan (1889-1958), Ireland's greatest opera singer, who was born nearby. The castle dates to the medieval period and was part of the Burke family's extensive holdings in Mayo.

name: Aille Castle
description: A small medieval tower on the wild Achill Island coast, surrounded by some of Ireland's most dramatic Atlantic scenery.
history: Aille Castle is a medieval tower on Achill Island, County Mayo. The castle stands in the wild coastal landscape of Ireland's largest island, surrounded by spectacular cliffs, beaches, and mountain scenery. Achill was home to a significant population throughout the medieval period despite its remote Atlantic location.

name: Owenduff Castle
description: Remote ruins near the Owenduff River in the wild boglands of northwest Mayo, one of the most isolated medieval sites in Ireland.
history: Owenduff Castle stands in the remote boglands of northwest Mayo near the Owenduff River. The castle dates to the medieval period and its extreme isolation reflects the scattered nature of settlement in this wild region. The Owenduff/Nephin Beg area is now one of Ireland's last remaining areas of intact Atlantic blanket bog.

name: Castlehill House
description: A Georgian mansion near Crossmolina in north Mayo, surrounded by parkland on the shores of Lough Conn.
history: Castlehill House near Crossmolina, County Mayo, is a Georgian country house on the shores of Lough Conn. The house was built by the Palmer family in the 18th century. It later served as an IDA training centre. The estate grounds overlook the scenic Lough Conn, one of Mayo's premier fishing lakes.

name: Errew Abbey
description: Evocative Augustinian ruins on a peninsula in Lough Conn, where medieval monks chose one of Mayo's most beautiful and secluded spots for prayer.
history: Errew Abbey on the shores of Lough Conn in north Mayo was an Augustinian foundation dating to the 13th century. The abbey occupies a beautiful lakeside peninsula. The ruins include a church with fine architectural details. Its remote setting on Lough Conn, surrounded by the mountains of north Mayo, makes it one of the most atmospherically sited monasteries in Ireland.

name: Templehouse Castle
description: A romantic lakeside estate in County Sligo, where a medieval Knights Templar castle stands beside a Georgian mansion on the shores of Templehouse Lake.
history: Templehouse near Ballymote, County Sligo, takes its name from a Knights Templar preceptory established here in the 12th century. The estate has been home to the Perceval family since the 17th century. The current Georgian house overlooks Templehouse Lake, with the ruins of the medieval castle nearby. The house operates as a guesthouse.

name: Markree Castle
description: Sligo's finest country house hotel, a magnificent Gothic Revival castle set in an ancient demesne along the banks of the River Unshin.
history: Markree Castle near Collooney, County Sligo, has been the seat of the Cooper family since 1663. The current Gothic Revival castle was substantially remodelled in the 1830s by architect Francis Johnston. The castle features a remarkable Great Hall and sits in 500 acres of parkland. It now operates as a luxury castle hotel.

name: Ardtermon Castle
description: A handsome fortified house on the wild Sligo coast near Mullaghmore, overlooking the Atlantic rollers of Donegal Bay.
history: Ardtermon Castle near Mullaghmore in County Sligo is a 17th-century fortified house in a dramatic coastal setting. The castle has been carefully maintained and restored. Its position on the Sligo coast, with views across Donegal Bay to the mountains of Donegal, makes it one of the most attractively sited historic houses in the northwest.

name: Lissadell House
description: The great Sligo house of the Gore-Booth sisters, immortalised by W.B. Yeats—a Greek Revival mansion where poetry, rebellion, and aristocratic idealism collided.
history: Lissadell House near Drumcliff, County Sligo, was home to the Gore-Booth family. Sisters Constance (later Countess Markievicz, the first woman elected to Westminster) and Eva (poet and suffragist) were immortalised by Yeats: 'The light of evening, Lissadell, Great windows open to the south.' The 1830s Greek Revival house has been restored and is open to visitors.

name: Kilglass Castle
description: A medieval tower in the Sligo countryside near Lough Gara, in a landscape rich with crannogs and ancient lake dwellings.
history: Kilglass Castle is a medieval tower house in County Sligo near Lough Gara. The lake area is renowned for its extraordinary concentration of crannogs (ancient lake dwellings), with over 350 identified. The castle dates to the medieval period and served as a local stronghold.

name: Banada Abbey
description: Well-preserved Augustinian friary ruins in the south Sligo countryside, featuring fine medieval architecture and a peaceful rural setting.
history: Banada Abbey in County Sligo was an Augustinian priory founded in the 15th century. The well-preserved ruins include a church with fine window tracery and other medieval architectural details. The abbey sits in a quiet rural setting in south Sligo, near the Ox Mountains.

name: Castlebaldwin
description: A small fortification in the Sligo village of Castlebaldwin, perched above the scenic Curlew Pass between Sligo and Roscommon.
history: Castlebaldwin in County Sligo takes its name from a medieval castle associated with the Baldwin family. The village sits near the Curlew Pass, a historically strategic defile between Sligo and Roscommon that was the site of several battles. The ruins are modest but the location is historically significant.

name: Collooney Castle
description: A modest tower in the Sligo town of Collooney, site of a significant 1798 battle where Franco-Irish forces defeated the British.
history: Collooney Castle is a medieval tower in County Sligo. The town is famous for the 1798 Battle of Collooney, where Bartholomew Teeling's charge during General Humbert's French expedition secured a rebel victory. A monument to Teeling stands in the town. The castle predates these events by centuries.

name: Skreen Castle
description: Ruins near the ancient ecclesiastical site of Skreen in County Sligo, where an early Christian monastery and medieval church preserve layers of sacred history.
history: Skreen in County Sligo has deep ecclesiastical roots, with monastic origins possibly dating to the 6th century. The castle remains and medieval church stand near the site of this early Christian foundation. The area was important in the spread of Christianity in the northwest and retains its heritage character.

name: Enniscrone Castle
description: Coastal castle remains in the Sligo seaside town famous for its seaweed baths, where Atlantic breakers crash against a long golden strand.
history: Enniscrone (Inishcrone) Castle in County Sligo stands near the town's famous seaweed baths and long sandy beach. The castle dates to the medieval period and guarded the coastline. Enniscrone is renowned for its traditional seaweed bathing experience, where visitors soak in hot seawater baths filled with fresh seaweed.

name: Carrowmore Megalithic Cemetery
description: One of the largest and oldest collections of megalithic tombs in Europe, a haunting Stone Age landscape overlooked by the summit cairn of Knocknarea.
history: Carrowmore near Sligo town contains over 30 passage tombs dating to around 3500 BC, making it one of Europe's oldest and largest megalithic cemeteries. The tombs predate Newgrange by several centuries. The site is dominated by views of Knocknarea mountain, crowned by Queen Maeve's cairn. W.B. Yeats drew deep inspiration from this ancient landscape.

name: Drumcliff Tower
description: A round tower stump and high cross at the burial place of W.B. Yeats, beneath the dramatic limestone cliff of Benbulben.
history: Drumcliff in County Sligo was the site of a 6th-century monastery founded by St Columcille. The remains include a round tower stump and a fine 11th-century high cross with biblical scenes. W.B. Yeats is buried in the churchyard here, his epitaph reading 'Cast a cold eye on life, on death. Horseman, pass by!' The dramatic backdrop of Benbulben mountain completes the scene.

name: Aclare Castle
description: A medieval tower in the south Sligo village of Aclare, in the shadow of the Ox Mountains.
history: Aclare Castle is a medieval tower house in County Sligo near the Ox Mountains. The castle dates to the medieval period and served as a local stronghold. The village of Aclare sits in the foothills between the Ox Mountains and the Curlew Hills.

name: Dromahair Castle
description: Ruins of an O'Rourke stronghold in the picturesque Leitrim village of Dromahair, where the legend of Devorgilla and her elopement changed Irish history.
history: Dromahair Castle was the principal stronghold of the O'Rourke clan, Kings of Breifne. The village is associated with the legendary elopement of Devorgilla O'Rourke with Dermot MacMurrough in 1152, which ultimately led MacMurrough to invite the Normans to Ireland—changing the course of Irish history forever. Nearby Creevelea Abbey adds to the heritage.

name: Drumshanbo Castle
description: Castle remains in the Leitrim lakeland town of Drumshanbo, gateway to the Shannon-Erne Waterway and the wild Leitrim uplands.
history: Drumshanbo Castle in County Leitrim stands in the town at the southern end of Lough Allen. The castle dates to the medieval period. Drumshanbo is now known as the home of Drumshanbo Gunpowder Irish Gin and sits at the junction of the Shannon-Erne Waterway and Lough Allen.

name: Carrick-on-Shannon Castle
description: Scant medieval remains in the charming Leitrim county town, now better known as Ireland's boating capital on the Shannon.
history: Carrick-on-Shannon, the county town of Leitrim, had a medieval castle guarding the Shannon crossing. Little remains of the original fortification, but the town has developed into a major Shannon boating and tourism centre. The tiny Costello Chapel, one of the smallest churches in Europe, is a notable attraction.

name: Leitrim Castle
description: Fragmentary remains of the castle that gave County Leitrim its name, near the banks of the Shannon in Ireland's least populated county.
history: Leitrim Castle, from which the county takes its name, stood near the village of Leitrim on the Shannon. The castle was an O'Rourke stronghold. County Leitrim remains Ireland's least populated county, preserving a rural character that has largely disappeared elsewhere. The castle site is of primarily historical rather than architectural interest.

name: Fenagh Abbey
description: Early Christian and medieval monastic ruins in the quiet Leitrim village of Fenagh, where ancient heritage centres preserve local history.
history: Fenagh Abbey in County Leitrim has monastic origins dating to the 6th century, associated with St Caillin. The surviving ruins include medieval church buildings. A local heritage centre preserves the history of this quiet village, which was once an important ecclesiastical site in the northwest.

name: Ballinagleragh Castle
description: A quiet ruin in the Leitrim lakelands, surrounded by the drumlins and small lakes that give this under-visited county its distinctive character.
history: Ballinagleragh Castle is a medieval fortification in County Leitrim. The castle dates to the medieval period and stands in the drumlin landscape typical of this part of Ireland. Leitrim's network of small lakes and hills creates a distinctive and peaceful rural landscape.

name: Jamestown Castle
description: Remains of a 17th-century Cromwellian fortification at a strategic Shannon crossing in County Leitrim.
history: Jamestown in County Leitrim was fortified in the 17th century as a strategic crossing point of the Shannon. The town was named after King James I and served as a garrison town. The fortifications were strengthened during the Cromwellian and Williamite periods. The town gate and some fortification elements survive.

name: Newtown Gore Castle
description: A tower in the Leitrim countryside near Newtowngore, in the quiet border country between Cavan and Leitrim.
history: Newtown Gore Castle is a medieval fortification near Newtowngore in County Leitrim. The castle stands in the border country between Cavan and Leitrim, an area of drumlins, small lakes, and quiet rural roads. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Mohill Castle
description: Castle remains in the Leitrim market town of Mohill, associated with the Leitrim family and the turbulent history of the northwest.
history: Mohill Castle in County Leitrim stands in the county's second-largest town. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with various local families. Mohill is notable as the home of Turlough O'Carolan (1670-1738), the legendary blind Irish harpist and composer, Ireland's national composer.

name: Drumlease Castle
description: A quiet ruin in the north Leitrim landscape, amid the lakes and mountains that inspired some of Ireland's greatest literary works.
history: Drumlease Castle is a medieval fortification in north County Leitrim. The area's lake-studded landscape inspired W.B. Yeats' famous poem 'The Lake Isle of Innisfree.' The castle dates to the medieval period and was part of the O'Rourke territory of Breifne.

name: Kiltyclogher Castle
description: Ruins in the border village of Kiltyclogher in north Leitrim, birthplace of the folk hero and IRA leader Sean MacDiarmada.
history: Kiltyclogher in north Leitrim has castle remains dating to the medieval period. The village is best known as the birthplace of Sean MacDiarmada, one of the seven signatories of the 1916 Proclamation who was executed after the Easter Rising. A heritage centre commemorates his life.

name: Doon Castle (Clare)
description: A lakeside tower house on the shores of Doon Lough in Clare, its quiet waters reflecting medieval walls against a backdrop of the Burren hills.
history: Doon Castle in County Clare stands on or near the shores of Doon Lough. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with local Clare families. The lakeside setting in the Clare countryside provides an atmospheric backdrop for the ruins.

name: Killone Abbey
description: Beautifully situated Augustinian nunnery ruins on the shores of Killone Lake near Ennis, one of the few medieval nunneries to survive in Ireland.
history: Killone Abbey near Ennis, County Clare, was an Augustinian nunnery founded in the 12th century by Donal Mor O'Brien, King of Thomond. It is one of the few surviving medieval nunneries in Ireland. The ruins are beautifully situated on the shores of Killone Lake and include a church with fine Romanesque details.

name: Termon Castle
description: A tower house in the Clare countryside, named for the ancient Irish concept of church sanctuary lands that once surrounded ecclesiastical sites.
history: Termon Castle in County Clare takes its name from 'termon' (sanctuary), indicating its location on former ecclesiastical lands. The castle dates to the medieval period and is one of Clare's many tower houses. The concept of termon lands—sanctuary territories surrounding churches and monasteries—was an important feature of early Irish law.

name: Castle Taylor
description: Ruins of a grand estate near Ardrahan in south Galway, once home to a prominent landowning family connected to Lady Gregory and the Irish Literary Revival.
history: Castle Taylor near Ardrahan, County Galway, was the seat of the Taylor family. The estate was connected to the circle of Lady Augusta Gregory, whose nearby Coole Park was the epicentre of the Irish Literary Revival. W.B. Yeats, J.M. Synge, and other literary figures visited the area. The castle fell into ruin during the 20th century.

name: Kilcogan Castle
description: A tower house near the south Galway coast, in the flat limestone landscape between Galway Bay and the Burren.
history: Kilcogan Castle is a medieval tower house in south County Galway near the coast. The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and was part of the network of fortified residences in the region between Galway city and the Burren. The area's flat limestone terrain is characteristic of the south Galway coast.

name: Ardfry Castle
description: Ruined Georgian mansion on the shores of Galway Bay, once seat of the Blake family and later a college, now a haunting shell overlooking the Atlantic.
history: Ardfry Castle near Oranmore, County Galway, was the seat of a branch of the Blake family, one of the Fourteen Tribes of Galway. The Georgian house overlooked Galway Bay. It later served as a university college before falling into ruin. The atmospheric shell stands in grounds with fine sea views.

name: Castle Hackett
description: A dramatic tower house crowning the summit of a rocky hill in south Galway, associated with the powerful Hackett family of medieval Connacht.
history: Castle Hackett in County Galway stands on a dramatic hilltop with commanding views. The castle was held by the Hackett family and later by the Kirwan family. The hilltop location on Castle Hackett Hill (Knockma) is associated in legend with the fairy king Finvarra. The tower dates to the medieval period.

name: Ballylee Castle
description: Thoor Ballylee—W.B. Yeats' beloved tower house, where Ireland's greatest poet wrote some of his finest work amid the wild beauty of south Galway.
history: Thoor Ballylee near Gort, County Galway, is a 16th-century Norman tower house purchased by W.B. Yeats in 1917 for £35. He restored it and lived here intermittently until 1929, writing masterworks including 'The Tower' and 'The Winding Stair.' The tower has been restored as a museum and literary shrine, though access has varied over the years.

name: Aughrim Castle
description: Ruins near the Galway village where the Battle of Aughrim in 1691—the bloodiest battle in Irish history—decided the fate of the Jacobite cause.
history: Aughrim in County Galway was the site of the Battle of Aughrim on 12 July 1691, the bloodiest battle ever fought on Irish soil, with an estimated 7,000 killed. The Williamite victory effectively ended the Jacobite War in Ireland. The castle ruins and an interpretive centre commemorate this pivotal event in Irish and European history.

name: Clonfert Cathedral
description: A tiny cathedral with the most ornate Romanesque doorway in Ireland—a riot of human heads, animal figures, and geometric patterns in ancient stone.
history: Clonfert Cathedral in County Galway preserves the finest Romanesque doorway in Ireland, dating to the 12th century. The extraordinary doorway features six orders of arches decorated with human heads, animal figures, chevrons, and foliage. The monastery was founded by St Brendan the Navigator in 563 AD. Though the cathedral is small, the doorway is an undisputed masterpiece of Irish medieval art.

name: Tuam Cathedral
description: A cathedral city in the heart of Galway, preserving a remarkable Romanesque chancel arch and high cross amid its medieval ecclesiastical heritage.
history: Tuam, County Galway, preserves the Cathedral of the Assumption with its magnificent 12th-century Romanesque chancel arch, one of the finest pieces of Romanesque architecture in Ireland. The town was the seat of the High Kings of Connacht and later an important archbishopric. A fine 12th-century high cross, the Cross of Tuam, also survives.

name: Corcomroe Abbey
description: A jewel of the Burren—a Cistercian abbey whose carved stonework blooms with wildflowers and human faces amid the grey limestone of County Clare.
history: Corcomroe Abbey in the Burren, County Clare, was founded around 1194 by Donal Mor O'Brien, King of Thomond. The Cistercian abbey is renowned for its exquisite stone carvings, which incorporate local flora—the carvings of flowers are so botanically accurate that the species can be identified. The abbey's setting in a Burren valley adds to its extraordinary atmosphere.

name: Kilfenora Cathedral
description: The medieval cathedral of the Burren, famous for its remarkable collection of high crosses and its unique status as a papal diocese.
history: Kilfenora Cathedral in County Clare dates to the 12th century and is famous for its collection of high crosses, including the remarkable Doorty Cross with its bishops' figures. Kilfenora is unique as a diocese technically still under the direct authority of the Pope—the only such parish in Ireland. The Burren Centre in the village explains the geology and heritage of the region.

name: Ballinvard Castle
description: A tower house ruin in the Waterford countryside, standing amid the rolling farmland of the Suir Valley.
history: Ballinvard Castle is a medieval tower house in County Waterford. The castle dates to the 15th century and was part of the network of fortified residences in the fertile Suir Valley. It served as a local stronghold for one of the area's landowning families.

name: Turoe Stone
description: A superbly carved La Tene Celtic stone in County Galway—one of the finest examples of Iron Age art in Europe, covered in flowing abstract designs.
history: The Turoe Stone near Loughrea, County Galway, is a granite boulder decorated with flowing La Tene (Celtic Iron Age) designs dating to around 300-100 BC. The abstract curvilinear patterns are among the finest examples of Celtic art in Europe. The stone was probably a ceremonial object. It now stands in the grounds of Turoe Pet Farm.

name: Moore Hall
description: A haunting lakeside ruin on the shores of Lough Carra, once home to the Moore family whose most famous son, George Moore, became a pioneering modernist novelist.
history: Moore Hall on the shores of Lough Carra, County Mayo, was home to the Moore family. George Moore (1852-1933) was a pioneering novelist and art critic who influenced the Irish Literary Revival. The house was burned during the Civil War in 1923. Its roofless shell on the lake shore is one of the most evocative Big House ruins in the west.

name: Westport House
description: One of Ireland's great country houses, a Palladian mansion built on the foundations of a Grace O'Malley castle, set in glorious grounds above Clew Bay.
history: Westport House, County Mayo, was built in the 1730s by architect Richard Cassels on the foundations of a castle belonging to Grace O'Malley. The house has been home to the Browne family (Marquesses of Sligo) for over 300 years. The magnificent interiors include a stunning dining room. The grounds feature gardens, a pirate adventure park, and views of Clew Bay.

name: Cloondooan Castle
description: A well-preserved tower house perched dramatically above the Atlantic in County Clare, where the cliffs meet the sea.
history: Cloondooan Castle is a well-preserved tower house on the Clare coast near Spanish Point. The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and stands in a dramatic cliff-edge position overlooking the Atlantic. Spanish Armada ships were wrecked nearby in 1588.

name: Murrisk Abbey
description: Atmospheric Augustinian friary ruins at the foot of Croagh Patrick, where pilgrims have gathered for millennia to climb Ireland's holiest mountain.
history: Murrisk Abbey on the shores of Clew Bay was an Augustinian friary founded in 1457 by the O'Malley family. The ruins stand at the traditional starting point for the pilgrimage up Croagh Patrick, Ireland's holiest mountain where St Patrick supposedly fasted for 40 days. A nearby sculpture commemorates the Famine coffin ships.

name: Strade Abbey
description: A Dominican friary in north Mayo preserving a remarkable collection of medieval tomb sculpture, including an extraordinary double-effigy tomb.
history: Strade Abbey near Foxford, County Mayo, was a Dominican friary. The abbey preserves a remarkable collection of medieval tomb sculptures, including a unique double-effigy tomb showing both a living figure and a cadaver beneath—a rare memento mori. The sculptural details are among the finest surviving medieval funerary art in the west of Ireland.

name: Creevelea Abbey
description: The last Franciscan friary founded in Ireland before the suppression, beautifully preserved in the wooded Leitrim countryside beside the Bonet River.
history: Creevelea Abbey near Dromahair, County Leitrim, was founded in 1508 by Owen O'Rourke and Margaret O'Brien—the last Franciscan friary established in Ireland before the dissolution. The well-preserved ruins include a fine cloister with carvings of St Francis and his animal companions. The riverside setting amid woodland is exceptionally beautiful.

name: Portumna Priory
description: Dominican priory ruins near Portumna Castle, adding medieval monastic heritage to this important Shannon-side town.
history: Portumna Priory was a Dominican foundation near Portumna, County Galway, on the shores of Lough Derg. The priory complements the nearby Portumna Castle and adds to the town's rich heritage as a major Shannon crossing point and lakeside settlement.

name: Granuaile Tower
description: A tower associated with Grace O'Malley on the Clare Island or mainland coast, commemorating the legendary pirate queen of the western seas.
history: Granuaile (Grace O'Malley) towers and castles are found at several points along the Mayo coast, marking the territory of Ireland's most famous female warrior. The pirate queen controlled the seas off the west coast in the 16th century, commanding a fleet from her various strongholds and famously negotiating with Queen Elizabeth I.

name: Ballykinavarga Castle
description: A tower house in the heart of the Burren, surrounded by the extraordinary karst landscape of County Clare.
history: Ballykinavarga Castle is a medieval tower house in the Burren, County Clare. The castle sits amid the unique limestone landscape of the Burren, with its characteristic pavement, wildflowers, and archaeological richness. The tower dates to the medieval period.

name: Craggaunowen Castle
description: A restored tower house housing a Bronze Age living history centre, where a full-size replica of St Brendan's leather boat proves the Atlantic was crossable 1,500 years ago.
history: Craggaunowen near Quin, County Clare, is a reconstructed heritage site centred on a restored 16th-century tower house. The site includes recreated crannogs, ring forts, and the Brendan Boat—a leather-hulled currach in which Tim Severin sailed to North America in 1976, proving St Brendan's legendary 6th-century voyage was technically possible.

name: Killaloe Cathedral
description: A fine medieval cathedral in the charming lakeside town of Killaloe, where Brian Boru held court as High King of Ireland.
history: St Flannan's Cathedral in Killaloe, County Clare, dates to the 12th century and stands near the site where Brian Boru, High King of Ireland, had his palace of Kincora. The cathedral preserves a remarkable Romanesque doorway and an ogham stone with both ogham and runic inscriptions. Killaloe sits at the southern end of Lough Derg on the Shannon.

name: Kilmacduagh Round Tower
description: Ireland's most dramatically leaning round tower, standing over 34 metres tall beside a remarkable cluster of medieval churches in the Galway countryside.
history: Kilmacduagh Monastery near Gort, County Galway, features Ireland's tallest leaning round tower at 34 metres, with a striking tilt of about half a metre. The monastic complex, founded by St Colman MacDuagh in the 7th century, includes a cathedral, several churches, and the tower. The setting, with the Burren hills behind, is one of Ireland's most photographed heritage scenes.

name: Roscam Round Tower
description: A round tower stump and early Christian church ruin on the shores of Galway Bay, with views across the water to the Burren.
history: Roscam Round Tower and church ruins stand on the shore of Galway Bay east of Galway city. The monastic site dates to the early Christian period and preserves the stump of a round tower and medieval church ruins. The waterside setting with views across Galway Bay to the Burren hills is particularly scenic.

name: Ballina Friary
description: Augustinian friary remains in the Mayo town of Ballina, on the banks of the salmon-rich River Moy.
history: Ballina Friary in County Mayo was an Augustinian foundation on the banks of the River Moy. Ballina is renowned as one of Ireland's premier salmon fishing towns, with the Moy being one of the most productive salmon rivers in Europe. The friary ruins add medieval heritage to this lively market town.

name: Classiebawn Castle
description: A dramatic Victorian castle on the Mullaghmore Peninsula in Sligo, tragically associated with the murder of Lord Mountbatten in 1979.
history: Classiebawn Castle on the Mullaghmore Peninsula, County Sligo, was built in the 1870s for Lord Palmerston. It later passed to the Mountbatten family. On 27 August 1979, Lord Louis Mountbatten was killed by an IRA bomb while fishing from his boat at Mullaghmore harbour. The castle remains in private hands and is visible from the coast road.

name: Ballyhannon Castle Clare
